Chapter 3: Importing & Exploring Data

R for Management Consultants | BK Advisors

BK Advisors

2026-02-28

Learning Objectives

After this chapter, you will be able to:

  • Import CSV files into R with read_csv() and specify column types
  • Import Excel files with read_excel(), selecting sheets, ranges, and skip rows
  • Explain the difference between data frames and tibbles
  • Explore datasets using head(), glimpse(), summary(), and other tools
  • Detect and handle missing values (NA) in facility-level data

Chapter Overview

Reading
CSV
→
Reading
Excel
→
Data Frames
& Tibbles
→
Exploring
Data
→
Handling
NAs

Reading CSV Files

Getting data from flat files into R

read_csv() Basics

read_csv() from the readr package reads comma-separated files into R as a tibble. It’s faster and smarter than base R’s read.csv() — it guesses column types, handles dates, and never converts strings to factors.

library(readr)

# Read a CSV file of facility-level MNH data
facility_data <- read_csv("facility_data.csv")

# View the result
facility_data

Always use read_csv() (with underscore) from readr, not read.csv() (with dot) from base R. It’s faster, produces tibbles, and gives you a helpful column specification message.

Specifying Column Types

Sometimes read_csv() guesses wrong — a facility ID like 001 becomes the number 1, or a yes/no column becomes text instead of logical.

facility_data <- read_csv(
  "facility_data.csv",
  col_types = cols(
    facility_id  = col_character(),   # Keep leading zeros
    country      = col_character(),
    facility_type = col_character(),
    delivery_volume = col_integer(),
    nmr          = col_double(),       # Neonatal mortality rate
    has_cpap     = col_logical()       # TRUE/FALSE
  )
)

DHIS2 exports often have facility codes with leading zeros. If you don’t specify col_character(), R will silently drop them — and your join keys won’t match later.

Common Import Problems

Problem Symptom Fix
Wrong delimiter All data in one column Use read_csv2() (semicolon) or read_delim()
Encoding issues Garbled characters (accents, special chars) Add locale = locale(encoding = "UTF-8")
No header row First data row used as column names Add col_names = FALSE or supply names
Metadata rows at top Junk rows before the real data Add skip = 3 (or however many rows)
Inconsistent NAs Missing values coded as “.”, “–”, “N/A” Add na = c("", "NA", ".", "--", "N/A") 
# A typical messy DHIS2 export
facility_data <- read_csv(
  "dhis2_export_2024.csv",
  skip = 2,                              # Skip metadata rows
  na = c("", "NA", ".", "--", "N/A"),    # Recognize all NA patterns
  locale = locale(encoding = "UTF-8")    # Handle accented names
)

Reading Excel Files

Because most health data still arrives in .xlsx

read_excel() Basics

read_excel() from the readxl package reads .xls and .xlsx files into R. It handles multiple sheets, can skip rows, and reads specific cell ranges — everything you need for messy ministry spreadsheets.

library(readxl)

# Read the first sheet
facility_data <- read_excel("mnh_indicators.xlsx")

# Read a specific sheet by name
delivery_data <- read_excel("mnh_indicators.xlsx", sheet = "Deliveries")

# Read a specific sheet by number
staffing_data <- read_excel("mnh_indicators.xlsx", sheet = 3)

read_excel() — Advanced Options

# Skip header rows (common in government reports)
facility_data <- read_excel(
  "ministry_report.xlsx",
  sheet = "FacilityData",
  skip = 4                    # Skip 4 rows of logos and titles
)

# Read a specific cell range
summary_table <- read_excel(
  "ministry_report.xlsx",
  sheet = "Summary",
  range = "B5:G25"            # Only read the data table
)

# List all sheet names (useful for unfamiliar files)
excel_sheets("ministry_report.xlsx")

Ministry of Health Excel files often have logos, merged cells, and title rows above the data. Use skip or range to jump past the decoration and land on the actual data.

CSV vs. Excel: When to Use Which

Use read_csv() when

  • Data is already in .csv format
  • DHIS2 or database exports
  • Large files (CSV is faster)
  • Sharing data between tools

Use read_excel() when

  • Data arrives as .xlsx from partners
  • Multiple sheets in one workbook
  • Government reports with formatting
  • You need a specific cell range

In consulting, you rarely choose the format. You get what the client sends. The skill is knowing which function to reach for and which arguments to set.

Data Frames & Tibbles

How R stores tabular data

What Is a Data Frame?

A data frame is R’s way of storing tabular data — rows and columns, like a spreadsheet. Every column is a vector, and all columns must have the same number of rows. A tibble is a modern, improved data frame from the tidyverse.

library(tibble)

# Create a tibble with MNH facility data
facility_data <- tibble(
  facility_id     = c("ETH-001", "ETH-002", "KEN-001", "MWI-001", "TZA-001"),
  country         = c("Ethiopia", "Ethiopia", "Kenya", "Malawi", "Tanzania"),
  facility_type   = c("Hospital", "Health Center", "Hospital", "Hospital", "Health Center"),
  delivery_volume = c(245, 58, 312, 189, 73),
  nmr             = c(28.5, NA, 19.2, 35.1, 22.8),
  has_cpap        = c(TRUE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE, FALSE)
)

facility_data

Tibble vs. Data Frame

Base data frame

# Prints ALL rows (overwhelming)
# Partial matching (dangerous)
df$coun  # returns "country"!
# Converts strings to factors

Tibble

# Prints only first 10 rows
# No partial matching (safer)
tbl$coun  # returns NULL + warning
# Keeps strings as strings

Tibbles are stricter — and that’s a feature, not a bug. They catch mistakes that base data frames silently ignore.

Accessing Columns

# Dollar sign notation — most common
facility_data$nmr

# Double bracket notation — useful in functions
facility_data[["nmr"]]

# Both return the same vector of NMR values
# Row 1, all columns
facility_data[1, ]

# All rows, columns 2 and 3
facility_data[, c(2, 3)]

# Rows 1-3, columns "country" and "nmr"
facility_data[1:3, c("country", "nmr")]

Use $ for interactive exploration and [[ ]] when writing functions. You’ll see both in MNH analysis scripts.

Exploring Your Data

The first thing you do after importing

The First Five Minutes with Any Dataset

Every time you import data, run these commands before doing anything else:

# How big is the data?
nrow(facility_data)   # Number of rows (facilities)
ncol(facility_data)   # Number of columns (variables)
dim(facility_data)    # Both at once: rows x columns
# Look at the first few rows
head(facility_data)         # First 6 rows (default)
head(facility_data, 10)     # First 10 rows

Always check the dimensions first. If you expected 500 facilities and got 12, something went wrong in the import — catch it now, not after an hour of analysis.

glimpse() — The Best First Look

library(dplyr)

# glimpse() shows every column, its type, and first values
glimpse(facility_data)

# Output looks like:
# Rows: 5
# Columns: 6
# $ facility_id     <chr> "ETH-001", "ETH-002", "KEN-001", "MWI-001", "TZA-001"
# $ country         <chr> "Ethiopia", "Ethiopia", "Kenya", "Malawi", "Tanzania"
# $ facility_type   <chr> "Hospital", "Health Center", "Hospital", "Hospital", ...
# $ delivery_volume <dbl> 245, 58, 312, 189, 73
# $ nmr             <dbl> 28.5, NA, 19.2, 35.1, 22.8
# $ has_cpap        <lgl> TRUE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE, FALSE

glimpse() is the single most useful exploration function. It shows you every column’s name, type, and sample values in one compact view. Make it your first instinct after every import.

summary() — Statistical Overview

summary(facility_data)

# For numeric columns, you get:
#   Min, 1st Quartile, Median, Mean, 3rd Quartile, Max, NA count
# For character columns:
#   Length, Class, Mode
# For logical columns:
#   Counts of TRUE/FALSE/NA

summary() reveals problems head() can’t. A max delivery volume of 999,999 is a data entry error. A median NMR of 0 means most facilities aren’t reporting. These red flags appear in summary(), not in the first six rows.

Exploration Cheat Sheet

Function What it tells you When to use it
nrow(), ncol() Dataset dimensions Always first
head() First few rows Quick sanity check
glimpse() Column names, types, samples After every import
summary() Min, max, median, NA counts Before analysis
names() Column names only When you need exact names
str() Structure (types + preview) Debugging type issues

In consulting, surprises in data are expensive. Spending five minutes exploring saves hours of debugging downstream. Make these commands a habit, not an afterthought.

Handling Missing Values

NA is not zero — and ignoring it is not a strategy

What Is NA?

NA stands for “Not Available.” It represents a missing or unknown value in R. It’s not zero, not blank, not an error — it’s R’s way of saying “we don’t know this value.”

# NA is contagious — any operation with NA returns NA
5 + NA            # NA
mean(c(10, 20, NA))  # NA (not 15!)

# You must explicitly tell R to ignore NAs
mean(c(10, 20, NA), na.rm = TRUE)  # 15

The most dangerous NAs are the ones you don’t notice. If mean(nmr) returns NA, your entire summary table is blank — and you won’t know why unless you check for missing values first.

Detecting Missing Values

# Count NAs per column
colSums(is.na(facility_data))

# Output:
# facility_id       0
# country           0
# facility_type     0
# delivery_volume   0
# nmr               1
# has_cpap          0

# Percentage missing per column
colMeans(is.na(facility_data)) * 100

# Output:
# facility_id       0
# country           0
# facility_type     0
# delivery_volume   0
# nmr              20
# has_cpap          0

What Missing Values Mean

Not all NAs are the same. Understanding why data is missing changes how you handle it:

Pattern Likely Meaning Example Action
NMR is NA Facility didn’t report Rural health post with no data clerk Investigate or exclude
delivery_volume is 0 Facility exists but had no deliveries Health post that refers all deliveries Keep — it’s real data
has_cpap is NA Equipment status unknown Survey question skipped Code as “Unknown”
Entire row is NA Empty row in source file Excel formatting artifact Remove the row
NMR is 0 Zero neonatal deaths reported Could be real or underreporting Verify with context

Zero and NA are different stories. A facility with 0 neonatal deaths had deliveries and reported no deaths. A facility with NA for NMR didn’t report at all. Treating NA as 0 inflates your denominator and biases your results downward.

Removing Missing Values

# Remove ALL rows with any NA (aggressive — use with caution)
clean_data <- na.omit(facility_data)

# Remove rows where a specific column is NA (targeted — preferred)
clean_data <- facility_data[!is.na(facility_data$nmr), ]
library(dplyr)

# Using dplyr filter (most readable)
clean_data <- facility_data |>
  filter(!is.na(nmr))

# Remove rows missing ANY of several key columns
clean_data <- facility_data |>
  filter(!is.na(nmr), !is.na(delivery_volume))

Prefer targeted removal over na.omit(). If one column has 40% missing values, na.omit() throws away 40% of your entire dataset — including rows that are perfectly fine for other analyses.

Working with NAs in Calculations

# Without na.rm, you get NA
mean(facility_data$nmr)                    # NA

# With na.rm = TRUE, NAs are excluded
mean(facility_data$nmr, na.rm = TRUE)      # 26.4

# Common summary functions that need na.rm
sum(facility_data$delivery_volume, na.rm = TRUE)
median(facility_data$nmr, na.rm = TRUE)
sd(facility_data$nmr, na.rm = TRUE)
min(facility_data$nmr, na.rm = TRUE)
max(facility_data$nmr, na.rm = TRUE)

Make na.rm = TRUE a habit. In MNH data, missing values are the norm, not the exception. Every mean(), sum(), and median() call should include it until you’ve explicitly confirmed there are no NAs.

Key Takeaways

Use read_csv() for CSV files and read_excel() for Excel files — specify column types and handle messy headers with skip, na, and col_types.

Always explore data immediately after import with glimpse(), summary(), and colSums(is.na()) — catch problems in the first five minutes, not after an hour of analysis.

NA is not zero. Handle missing values deliberately with na.rm = TRUE in calculations and targeted filtering with filter(!is.na()) rather than blanket na.omit().

Looking Ahead

Next: Chapter 4 — Data Transformation with dplyr

  • The pipe operator (|>) for readable workflows
  • filter(), select(), mutate() — the core data verbs
  • group_by() + summarise() — R’s answer to pivot tables
